flashcards.Back: An open-standard link-state routing protocol for IPv4. it uses the Shortest Path First (SPF) algorithm to calculate loop-free paths within an autonomous system.
A unique 32-bit number identifying an OSPF router. it is determined by: 1. Manual config, 2. Highest working loopback IP, 3. Highest working physical interface IP.
The backbone area in an OSPF multi-area design. In a single-area design, all OSPF-enabled interfaces must be assigned to Area 0.
A sequence of bits used in the 'network' command to specify which interfaces should participate in OSPF. a '0' bit means 'match' and a '1' bit means 'ignore'.
A relationship between two OSPF routers on the same link that have exchanged Hello packets and agreed on essential parameters like timers and area IDs.
A multicast packet (224.0.0.5) sent periodically to discover neighbors, maintain adjacencies, and elect the Designated Router.
The timer (default 10s on Ethernet) that defines how often an OSPF router sends Hello packets. neighbors must have matching intervals to form an adjacency.
The time a router waits (default 40s) without hearing a Hello packet from a neighbor before declaring that neighbor down.
A router elected on a multi-access network (like Ethernet) to manage the LSDB synchronization, reducing the number of OSPF adjacencies on the segment.
A router that stands ready to take over the DR role immediately if the primary DR fails, ensuring continuous routing updates.
An OSPF router on a multi-access segment that is neither the DR nor the BDR. DROTHERs only form full adjacencies with the DR and BDR.
The final state of an OSPF adjacency, indicating that the neighbors have completely synchronized their link-state databases.
Dijkstra's algorithm. it calculates the shortest, loop-free path from the local router to every destination in the LSDB based on cumulative cost.
A value assigned to an interface based on its bandwidth. Formula: $Cost = \frac{Reference\ Bandwidth}{Interface\ Bandwidth}$. lower cost is preferred.
The numerator in the OSPF cost formula, default 100 Mbps. it must be increased on modern networks to distinguish between 1 Gbps and 10 Gbps links.
The interface-level command used to manually override the calculated OSPF cost for a specific link.
A configuration that stops a router from sending/receiving OSPF Hellos on an interface, while still advertising the interface's subnet to the rest of the network.
A virtual interface often used as an OSPF Router ID because it is more stable than physical ports (it never goes down unless the router does).
A complete map of all routers and links in an OSPF area. every router in the area must have an identical copy of the LSDB.
A data packet containing information about a router's local links. routers flood LSAs throughout the area to build and update the LSDB.
A locally significant number used to start the OSPF routing process. it does not need to match between neighboring routers.
The primary verification command to check the status of adjacencies, showing the neighbor ID, state (like FULL), and local interface.
A concise verification command displaying which interfaces are participating in OSPF, their area, cost, and DR/BDR status.
An OSPF neighbor state indicating that bidirectional communication has been established, but the LSDBs have not yet been synchronized.
An OSPF packet type used to flood one or more LSAs to neighbors during the synchronization process.
A packet (LSAck) sent to confirm that an LSU was received, ensuring reliable delivery of topology updates.
The administrative distance for OSPF is 110, placing it between EIGRP (90) and RIP (120) in terms of trustworthiness.
A value (0–255) configured on an interface. The router with the highest priority becomes the DR. setting it to 0 prevents a router from ever becoming DR/BDR.
The default network type for Ethernet interfaces, where a DR and BDR are elected to manage adjacencies.
A network type (like HDLC or PPP) where exactly two routers are connected. No DR/BDR election occurs, and Hellos are sent to 224.0.0.5.
